title: The Ratio, Root, and Comparison Tests

epigraph.text:
A just balance and scales are the Lord's; all the weights 
in the bag are his work.

content.text:
The Integral Test is powerful but requires computing an integral. The tests
in this section are often easier to apply: they compare our series to known
series, or examine the ratio or root of consecutive terms.

The idea is simple: if we can compare our series term-by-term with a known
series, we can conclude convergence or divergence.

content.title: Direct Comparison Test
content.content:
Let $\sum a_{n}$ and $\sum b_{n}$ be series with $a_{n}, b_{n} \geq 0$ for
all $n$.

1. If $a_{n} \leq b_{n}$ for all $n$ and $\sum b_{n}$ converges, then
 $\sum a_{n}$ converges.

2. If $a_{n} \geq b_{n}$ for all $n$ and $\sum b_{n}$ diverges, then
 $\sum a_{n}$ diverges.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
In short: **smaller than convergent is convergent; bigger than
divergent is divergent.**

content.title: Direct Comparison: convergence
content.solution:
For $n \geq 1$: $n^{2} + 1 > n^{2}$, so $\dfrac{1}{n^{2}+1} < \dfrac{1}{n^{2}}$.

Since $\sum \frac{1}{n^{2}}$ converges ($p$-series with $p = 2 > 1$), the
Direct Comparison Test tells us $\sum \frac{1}{n^{2}+1}$ **converges**.

content.title: Direct Comparison: divergence
content.solution:
For $n \geq 3$: $\ln n \geq 1$, so $\dfrac{\ln n}{n} \geq \dfrac{1}{n}$.

Since $\sum \frac{1}{n}$ diverges (harmonic series), the Direct Comparison
Test tells us $\sum \frac{\ln n}{n}$ **diverges**.

content.title: Comparison with geometric series
content.solution:
For $n \geq 1$: $3^{n} + n > 3^{n}$, so $\dfrac{1}{3^{n}+n} < \dfrac{1}{3^{n}}$.

Since $\sum \frac{1}{3^{n}}$ converges (geometric with $r = 1/3 < 1$), the
series $\sum \frac{1}{3^{n}+n}$ **converges**.

content.text:
Sometimes finding an exact inequality is awkward. The Limit Comparison Test
handles cases where terms have the "same order of magnitude."

content.title: Limit Comparison Test
content.content:
Let $\sum a_{n}$ and $\sum b_{n}$ be series with $a_{n}, b_{n} > 0$ for all $n$.
If
\[
 \limto{n\to\infty}\frac{a_{n}}{b_{n}} = c,
\]
where $0 < c < \infty$, then $\sum a_{n}$ and $\sum b_{n}$ either both
converge or both diverge.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Limit Comparison Test
Limit Comparison: divergence
Choose the comparison series wisely
content.content:
The Limit Comparison Test requires a comparison series $\sum b_{n}$ whose
convergence you already know. Typically, use the dominant term(s) of $a_{n}$
to guide your choice. If $a_{n} \approx \frac{p(n)}{q(n)}$ for polynomials
$p$ and $q$, compare to $\frac{n^{\deg p}}{n^{\deg q}} = n^{\deg p - \deg q}$.

content.text:
The Ratio Test is particularly effective for series involving factorials,
exponentials, or powers.

content.title: The Ratio Test
content.content:
Let $\sum a_{n}$ be a series with $a_{n} > 0$. Define
\[
 L = \limto{n\to\infty}\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}.
\]

1. If $L < 1$, the series **converges**.

2. If $L > 1$ (or $L = \infty$), the series **diverges**.

3. If $L = 1$, the test is **inconclusive**.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $L < 1$, choose $r$ with $L < r < 1$. Then for sufficiently large $n$,
$a_{n+1}/a_{n} < r$, so terms eventually decrease geometrically. The tail
of the series is bounded by a convergent geometric series.

If $L > 1$, then $a_{n+1} > a_{n}$ for large $n$, so $a_{n} \not\to 0$, and
the series diverges by the Divergence Test.

content.title: Ratio Test: convergence
Ratio Test: divergence
Ratio Test: inconclusive
content.solution:
Let $a_{n} = \frac{1}{n^{2}}$. Then:
\[
 \frac{a_{n+1}}{a_{n}}
 = \frac{1/(n+1)^{2}}{1/n^{2}}
 = \frac{n^{2}}{(n+1)^{2}}.
\]
Thus $L = \limto{n\to\infty}\frac{n^{2}}{(n+1)^{2}} = 1$.

The Ratio Test is **inconclusive**. (We know from the $p$-series test
that this series converges.)

content.title: The Ratio Test often fails for algebraic terms
content.content:
When $a_{n}$ involves only powers of $n$ (no factorials or exponentials),
the Ratio Test typically gives $L = 1$. Use Comparison or the Integral Test
instead.

content.text:
The Root Test is similar to the Ratio Test but examines $\sqrt[n]{a_{n}}$
instead of $a_{n+1}/a_{n}$.

content.title: The Root Test
content.content:
Let $\sum a_{n}$ be a series with $a_{n} \geq 0$. Define
\[
 L = \limto{n\to\infty}\sqrt[n]{a_{n}}.
\]

1. If $L < 1$, the series **converges**.

2. If $L > 1$ (or $L = \infty$), the series **diverges**.

3. If $L = 1$, the test is **inconclusive**.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Root Test: convergence
content.solution:
Let $a_{n} = \left(\frac{n}{2n+1}\right)^{n}$. Then:
\[
 \sqrt[n]{a_{n}} = \frac{n}{2n+1}.
\]
Thus $L = \limto{n\to\infty}\frac{n}{2n+1} = \frac{1}{2} < 1$.

By the Root Test, the series **converges**.

content.title: Root Test: divergence
content.solution:
Let $a_{n} = \left(\frac{n+1}{n}\right)^{n^{2}}
= \left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n^{2}}$. Then:
\[
 \sqrt[n]{a_{n}} = \left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}.
\]
We know $\limto{n\to\infty}\left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n} = e \approx 2.718 > 1$.

By the Root Test, the series **diverges**.

content.title: Comparing Root and Ratio Tests
content.problem:
Determine whether $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{2^{n}}$ converges
using both tests.
content.solution:
Let $a_{n} = \frac{1}{2^{n}}$.

**Ratio Test:**
$\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_{n}} = \frac{1/2^{n+1}}{1/2^{n}} = \frac{1}{2}$, so $L = \frac{1}{2} < 1$. Converges.

**Root Test:**
$\sqrt[n]{a_{n}} = \sqrt[n]{1/2^{n}} = \frac{1}{2}$, so $L = \frac{1}{2} < 1$. Converges.

Both tests work equally well here.

content.text:
With several tests available, how do you choose? Here are practical
guidelines:

content.title: Choosing a Convergence Test
content.content:
1. **Divergence Test first.** If $a_{n} \not\to 0$, the series
 diverges.

2. **Recognize standard forms.**
 itemize[itemsep=2pt]

3. Geometric: $\sum ar^{n}$ — use the geometric series formula.

4. $p$-series: $\sum 1/n^{p}$ — converges iff $p > 1$.

5. Telescoping: partial fractions may reveal cancellation.
 itemize

6. **Factorials or exponentials?** Use the **Ratio Test**.
 Factorials grow faster than any exponential.

7. **$n$th power in exponent?** Use the **Root Test**.

8. **Rational function of $n$?** Use **Limit Comparison**
 with a $p$-series.

9. **Decreasing positive terms?** Consider the **Integral
 Test**.

10. **Easy inequality available?** Use **Direct Comparison**.

content.title: Strategy practice
content.problem:
Determine convergence or divergence for each series:

**(a)** $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{n!}{10^{n}}$

**(b)** $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{5n-2}{n^{3}+n}$

**(c)** $\displaystyle\sum_{n=2}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^{2}-1}$
content.solution:
**(a)** Factorial present — use Ratio Test.
\[
 \frac{a_{n+1}}{a_{n}} = \frac{(n+1)!/10^{n+1}}{n!/10^{n}}
 = \frac{n+1}{10} \to \infty > 1.
\]
**Diverges** by Ratio Test.

**(b)** Rational in $n$ — use Limit Comparison with $b_{n} = 1/n^{2}$.
\[
 \frac{a_{n}}{b_{n}} = \frac{(5n-2)n^{2}}{n^{3}+n}
 = \frac{5n^{3}-2n^{2}}{n^{3}+n} \to 5.
\]
Since $\sum 1/n^{2}$ converges, the series **converges**.

**(c)** Telescoping candidate: $\frac{1}{n^{2}-1} = \frac{1}{(n-1)(n+1)}
= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{n-1} - \frac{1}{n+1}\right)$.

Partial sum telescopes:
$s_{N} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{N} - \frac{1}{N+1}\right)
\to \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{3}{2} = \frac{3}{4}$.

**Converges** to $\frac{3}{4}$.

marginNotes.text:
Iron sharpens iron, and one man sharpens
another.

In the day of prosperity be joyful, and
in the day of adversity consider: God has made the one as well as the other.

For to everyone who has will more be given,
and he will have an abundance. But from the one who has not, even what he
has will be taken away.

But where shall wisdom be found? And where is
the place of understanding?

marginNotes.author: Richard Courant
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics as an expression of the human mind
reflects the active will, the contemplative reason, and the desire for
aesthetic perfection.

marginNotes.author: John von Neumann
marginNotes.text:
In mathematics you don't understand things.
You just get used to them.

marginNotes.author: George Pólya
marginNotes.text:
The first rule of discovery is to have brains and
good luck. The second rule of discovery is to sit tight and wait till you
get a bright idea.
